smooth-projective-curve.title: Smooth projective curves and their genus

questions.q: Let $C$ be a smooth projective curve of genus $g = 5$ over $\bar k$. What is $\dim_k H^0(C, \Omega^1_C)$?
questions.explain: By definition the genus equals $h^0(\Omega^1_C)$. So $\dim H^0(C, \Omega^1_C) = g = 5$.
questions.hint: The genus is the dimension of the space of regular 1-forms — that's the definition.

questions.q: A smooth projective curve $C(\mathbb{C})$ has topological Euler characteristic $\chi_{\mathrm{top}} = -8$. What is its genus $g$?
A degree-3 cover $\pi\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$ has $b = 8$ simple branch points (each contributing $1$ to the ramification sum). Compute the genus of $C$ by Riemann–Hurwitz.
questions.hint: $2g - 2 = d(2 g_{\mathbb{P}^1} - 2) + b$ for simple branching.

hard.q: Which of the following is FALSE for a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g$ over an algebraically closed field?
choices: Every divisor class of degree $> 2g - 2$ has $h^1 = 0$.
$C$ admits a non-constant map to $\mathbb{P}^1$ of degree $\le \lfloor (g+3)/2 \rfloor$ (gonality bound).
Every curve of genus $g \ge 2$ admits a degree-$2$ map to $\mathbb{P}^1$.
hard.explain: Only hyperelliptic curves have a $g^1_2$ — for $g \ge 3$ the generic curve is non-hyperelliptic. The Brill–Noether-style gonality bound $\lfloor (g+3)/2 \rfloor$ is correct.
hard.hint: Most curves of genus $\ge 3$ do NOT admit a degree-2 map to $\mathbb{P}^1$.

questions.q: On a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g = 4$ over $\mathbb{C}$, what is the complex dimension of the Jacobian $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$?
Let $D = 2[p] - 3[q] + [r]$ on a smooth projective curve. Compute $\deg D$.
Two divisors $D, D'$ on a smooth projective curve are linearly equivalent iff:
choices: $D - D' = \mathrm{div}(f)$ for some nonzero rational function $f \in k(C)^\times$.
questions.hint: Linear equivalence is by definition: $D \sim D'$ iff $D - D'$ is a principal divisor, i.e.
questions.explain: Linear equivalence is by definition: $D \sim D'$ iff $D - D'$ is a principal divisor, i.e. the divisor of some rational function. This is the equivalence relation defining $\mathrm{Pic}(C)$.

questions.q: On a smooth projective curve of genus $g = 3$, take $D$ with $\deg D = 5$. What is $h^0(D)$? (Use the high-degree vanishing: $\deg D > 2g - 2$.)
questions.explain: $\deg D = 5 > 2g - 2 = 4$, so $h^0(K - D) = 0$ and Riemann–Roch gives $h^0(D) = \deg D - g + 1 = 5 - 3 + 1 = 3$.
questions.hint: Past the threshold $\deg D > 2g-2$, $h^0(D) = \deg D - g + 1$.

questions.q: On a curve of genus $g = 2$, $D = K_C$ (the canonical divisor). Compute $h^0(D)$.
questions.hint: $h^0(K_C) = g = 2$ — this is the definition of genus restated, and Riemann–Roch with $D = K$ recovers it: $h^0(K) - h^0(0) = (2g-2) - g + 1 = g - 1$, with $h^0(0) = 1$ giving $h^0(K) = g$.
questions.explain: $h^0(K_C) = g = 2$ — this is the definition of genus restated, and Riemann–Roch with $D = K$ recovers it: $h^0(K) - h^0(0) = (2g-2) - g + 1 = g - 1$, with $h^0(0) = 1$ giving $h^0(K) = g$.

questions.q: On a curve of genus $g = 4$, take $D$ with $\deg D = 3$. Suppose further that $h^0(K - D) = 2$. What is $h^0(D)$?
questions.hint: Just plug $\deg D = 3$, $g = 4$, $h^0(K-D) = 2$ into RR and solve for $h^0(D)$.

hard.q: On a non-hyperelliptic genus-$3$ curve $C$, let $D = p + q + r$ with $p, q, r$ three distinct points whose canonical-embedding images are NOT collinear. Riemann–Roch then gives $h^0(D) = 1$. Compute $h^0(K - D)$.
hard.explain: Riemann–Roch: $h^0(D) - h^0(K - D) = \deg D - g + 1 = 3 - 3 + 1 = 1$. With $h^0(D) = 1$ this gives $h^0(K - D) = 0$. Equivalently: $K - D$ has degree $4 - 3 = 1$, but the three points are not collinear in $\mathbb{P}^2$, so no canonical hyperplane (= line in the canonical $\mathbb{P}^2$) cuts out $D$, and $|K - D|$ is empty.

hard.q: On a curve $C$ of genus $g$, the linear system $|D|$ defines a morphism $\varphi_D\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^{r}$ where $r = h^0(D) - 1$. For $|D|$ to give a closed embedding, the standard sufficient condition is:
hard.explain: On a smooth projective curve, $\deg D \ge 2g + 1$ guarantees very ample (and $\ge 2g$ guarantees base-point-free + injective on tangent vectors generically). The standard cleanest threshold is $2g + 1$ ⇒ very ample, so 'at least $2g$' is the correct intuition. Of the four options, (a) is the canonical sufficient condition for very ampleness.

questions.q: On a smooth projective curve of genus $g = 5$, what is $\deg K_C$?
If $C$ is a non-hyperelliptic smooth projective curve of genus $g = 4$, into what projective space $\mathbb{P}^N$ does the canonical map embed $C$? Give $N$.
A smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g \ge 3$ is non-hyperelliptic if and only if:
choices: the canonical map $\varphi_K\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^{g-1}$ is a closed embedding.
questions.hint: Max Noether/Enriques–Babbage: $\varphi_K$ is an embedding ⇔ $C$ is non-hyperelliptic (assuming $g \ge 2$).
questions.explain: Max Noether/Enriques–Babbage: $\varphi_K$ is an embedding ⇔ $C$ is non-hyperelliptic (assuming $g \ge 2$). For hyperelliptic $C$, $\varphi_K$ factors through the $2:1$ map to $\mathbb{P}^1$ followed by the rational normal curve.

hard.q: Which of the following is the correct genus-$3$ canonical model dichotomy?
choices: Every genus-3 curve embeds canonically as a smooth quartic in $\mathbb{P}^2$.
Non-hyperelliptic genus-3 ⇒ smooth quartic in $\mathbb{P}^2$; hyperelliptic genus-3 ⇒ canonical image is a smooth conic, mapped $2:1$.
Every genus-3 curve embeds canonically in $\mathbb{P}^3$ as a (2, 3) complete intersection.
Genus-3 curves never have a canonical embedding.
hard.explain: For $g = 3$: non-hyperelliptic curves embed as smooth plane quartics ($\deg = 2g-2 = 4$ in $\mathbb{P}^{g-1} = \mathbb{P}^2$); hyperelliptic curves have $\varphi_K$ factoring through $C \xrightarrow{2:1} \mathbb{P}^1 \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^2$ as a smooth conic. Choice (c) is the genus-4 dichotomy.

hard.q: A smooth canonical curve of genus $g = 6$ in $\mathbb{P}^5$ has degree:

questions.q: How many branch points does a hyperelliptic cover $\pi\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$ have when $C$ has genus $g = 5$?
questions.explain: For a hyperelliptic curve of genus $g$, the degree-$2$ cover has $2g + 2$ branch points (Riemann–Hurwitz with $d = 2$, simple ramification: $2g - 2 = 2(-2) + b \Rightarrow b = 2g + 2$). For $g = 5$: $b = 12$.

questions.q: Select all curves which are hyperelliptic. (Assume each is smooth projective over $\bar k$ and the equations are written affinely.)
questions.explain: (a) $y^2 = $ (degree 5) is hyperelliptic of genus 2. (c) $y^2 = $ (degree 7) is hyperelliptic of genus 3. (d) every genus-2 curve is hyperelliptic (canonical map is the unique $g^1_2$). The smooth plane quartic in (b) is the canonical model of a non-hyperelliptic genus-3 curve (generic case).
questions.hint: Hyperelliptic ⇔ $y^2 = f(x)$ with $f$ separable of degree $2g+1$ or $2g+2$, OR genus $\le 2$.

questions.q: On a hyperelliptic curve of genus $g = 4$ with hyperelliptic involution $\sigma$, how many fixed points does $\sigma$ have on $C$?
questions.explain: Fixed points of $\sigma$ are exactly the ramification points of $\pi\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$, which sit one above each branch point. Number = $2g + 2 = 10$.
questions.hint: Ramification points = fixed points of involution = $2g + 2$.

questions.q: What is the codimension of the hyperelliptic locus $\mathcal{H}_g \subset \mathcal{M}_g$ for $g = 5$?
questions.explain: $\mathcal{H}_g$ has dimension $2g - 1$ inside $\mathcal{M}_g$ of dimension $3g - 3$, so codimension $(3g-3) - (2g-1) = g - 2$. For $g = 5$: codim $= 3$.

questions.q: The Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ adds boundary divisors $\Delta_0, \Delta_1, \ldots, \Delta_{\lfloor g/2 \rfloor}$. The divisor $\Delta_0$ parametrises:
choices: irreducible nodal curves: a single component of geometric genus $g - 1$ with one self-intersecting node.
the union of two smooth curves of genera $0$ and $g$ glued at one node.
questions.explain: $\Delta_0$ is the boundary divisor of irreducible nodal curves: pinch a non-separating loop in $C$ to a node, get an irreducible curve whose normalisation has genus $g - 1$. The arithmetic genus stays $g$.
questions.hint: Pinch a non-separating loop on $C$ — what stays connected?

hard.q: For $g = 5$, how many distinct boundary divisors $\Delta_i$ does $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ have? (Count all $\Delta_i$ with $0 \le i \le \lfloor g/2 \rfloor$.)
hard.explain: Boundary divisors are $\Delta_0, \Delta_1, \Delta_2$ (since $\lfloor 5/2 \rfloor = 2$ and $\Delta_i$ for $i = 0, 1, 2$ are distinct: $\Delta_0$ irreducible nodal, $\Delta_1$ the (1, 4)-split, $\Delta_2$ the (2, 3)-split). Total $= 3$.

hard.q: The codimension of any single boundary divisor $\Delta_i \subset \overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ is:
hard.explain: By definition / construction $\Delta_i$ is a boundary divisor — codimension $1$. Pinching $k$ disjoint cycles produces a stratum of codimension $k$; pinching exactly one cycle (the generic point of $\Delta_i$) produces codim 1.
